content:
Ryan, here’s what I’d do—with a hand on my 10-year-old’s shoulder while Psalm 19 is read.

- During the reading, quietly point out two anchors:
  - Verses 1–6: “The heavens declare the glory of God.” That’s true truth, even though it’s said in poetry. Poetry uses pictures (the sun “like a bridegroom,” “like a strong man”) to tell us something real: creation shouts that God is glorious.
  - Verses 7–11: “The law of the LORD is perfect.” God’s Word isn’t just beautiful; it’s flawless, trustworthy, right, clear, pure, and true.

- After the reading, whisper something simple:
  - “See how God’s world tells the truth about Him, and God’s Word tells the truth about everything we need. Poetry can be full of pictures and still tell the truth. That’s how the Bible works.”

- Later that day at home, connect Psalm 19 to Genesis 1:
  - Open Psalm 19 and show the child the pictures (similes, metaphors).
  - Open Genesis 1 and show the repeated, steady refrains: “And God said… and it was so… and God saw that it was good… and there was evening and morning, the nth day.” That’s narrative cadence—still beautiful, but reporting what God did.
  - Explain genres simply: “Poetry paints; stories report. Both are true when God speaks.”
  - Hold out the main non-negotiables the Bible makes plain:
    - God made everything from nothing by His word (Hebrews 11:3).
    - He did it with order, purpose, and goodness (Genesis 1).
    - Humanity begins with a real Adam and Eve (Romans 5; 1 Corinthians 15).
  - Acknowledge that faithful Christians differ on how to understand the ‘days,’ but none of that gives us permission to treat God’s Word as less than perfect. We read each passage as it presents itself, and we never call truth “a stumbling block” just to make peace.

- Use Psalm 19 to teach how to read the Bible:
  - Poetry: “The sun runs its course.” That’s not bad science; it’s normal human description and imagery. It’s still true.
  - Narrative: “God said… and there was.” That’s God telling us what happened.
  - Bottom line for a 10-year-old: “When the Bible uses pictures, we enjoy the pictures. When it tells the story, we believe the story. In both, God never lies.”

- Pray verse 14 together: “Let the words of our mouths and the meditation of our hearts be acceptable…” Ask God to make your family love both His world and His Word.

As for the elder, I’d follow up gently and plainly:
- Thank him for caring about unity.
- Ask what he means by “poetic.” If he means “non-historical,” explain you can’t agree, because Scripture’s authority doesn’t let us treat Genesis 1 as less than real reporting simply to lower friction—especially when Exodus 20:11 roots Sabbath in God’s six-day work and one-day rest.
- Suggest a healthier path to unity:
  - Teach the congregation how genres communicate truth.
  - Define the doctrinal boundaries clearly (Creator/creation ex nihilo, historic Adam and Eve, goodness of creation, reality of the fall).
  - Allow charitable space where Scripture allows it, without shaming those who read Genesis 1 as straightforward history.

Unity is precious, but it must be unity in the truth. Psalm 19 actually helps here: God’s world shouts His glory, and God’s Word is perfect. I want my child to learn both at once—and to see that we don’t trim Scripture to fit us; we bow to it in love.
